title: An Alphabet of Tales
summary: The repository XML is the pinned CME copy of a 1997 University of Michigan electronic text derived from Mary Macleod Banks's complete two-part EETS edition (1904-1905). Banks directly transcribed the anonymous fifteenth-century Northern Middle English translation in British Library Add MS 25719 and compared difficult readings with the Latin Alphabetum narrationum in Harley MS 268 and Arundel MS 378. The Latin source is now attributed to Arnold of Liege rather than Etienne de Besancon. Complete scans of both print volumes and the principal supporting scholarship are cached locally. Part II preserves one reduced facsimile of Add MS 25719, f. 131r, but no public complete manuscript facsimile was verified; the manifest therefore records a whole-codex British Library reproduction route and maps the work to ff. 1r-184v within the manuscript.
review_notes: The cataloging subject is the Middle English translation, not Arnold of Liege's Latin compilation, Banks's edition, or the manuscript as a physical object.
Etienne de Besancon is retained only as a rejected historical attribution and as part of the 1904 bibliographic title.
Harley MS 268 and Arundel MS 378 are Banks's explicitly named Latin comparison copies; they are not asserted to be the anonymous medieval translator's exemplar.
Add MS 25719 contains the English work at ff. 1r-184v and a distinct service-book flyleaf at f. 185. A future facsimile request should cover the entire physical codex, not only the work leaves.
Banks's 1905 Part II frontispiece reproduces only Add MS 25719, f. 131r. It is useful paleographical evidence but must not be presented as a complete manuscript facsimile.
A blank Digitised Content field and negative public searches are date-bounded evidence, not proof that no private, internal, or unindexed surrogate exists.

entities.label: General Corpus source XML for AlphTales
entities.description: The repository XML consumed by the General Corpus conversion workflow and supplied through the pinned CME submodule.

entities.label: University of Michigan CME item AlphTales
entities.description: A 1997 electronic edition of Banks's two printed parts treated as one text. Sencor keyboarded the text; after a five-percent proof failed U-M's stated one-in-20,000-character standard, Jean Borger and David Westbrook completely proofed and corrected it, and Ken Church reviewed it.
entities.reproduction_format: Keyboarded, proofed, and structurally marked-up electronic text with 532 print-page milestones
notes: The XML considers the 1904 and 1905 parts one text, moves both editorial notes to the front, and encodes the printed errata without silently applying them.

entities.label: Banks's An Alphabet of Tales
alternate_labels: An Alphabet of Tales, Part I, A-H
An Alphabet of Tales, Part II, I-Z
entities.description: The immediate print source: a complete two-part transcription of Add MS 25719. Banks restores worn or obliterated text conjecturally in brackets, distinguishes inadvertently omitted supplies with bracketed italics, modernizes punctuation, selectively regularizes capitalization, corrects scribal errors in footnotes, and compares difficult readings with Harley MS 268 and Arundel MS 378.

bibliographic.citation: An Alphabet of Tales: An English 15th Century Translation of the Alphabetum Narrationum of Etienne de Besancon, from Additional MS. 25,719 of the British Museum, ed. Mary Macleod Banks, 2 parts, EETS Original Series 126-127 (London: Kegan Paul, Trench, Trubner & Co., 1904-1905).
bibliographic.title: An Alphabet of Tales
bibliographic.series: Early English Text Society, Original Series 126-127
bibliographic.publisher: Kegan Paul, Trench, Trubner & Co., Ltd.
bibliographic.pages: [v] + 532 pages in two parts, plus errata

notes: Part I covers A-H and printed pages 1-260; Part II covers I-Z and pages 261-532.
Part II includes a reduced photographic facsimile of Add MS 25719, f. 131r, as its frontispiece; this is a single selected manuscript leaf, not a complete manuscript reproduction.
The promised Part III introduction, glossary, index, and general clearing-up was not included in these two parts and was not found as a later publication.
Part II changes the subtitle to 'once attributed to Etienne de Besancon' after Herbert's 1905 authorship article.

entities.label: Internet Archive scans of Banks's two-part edition
entities.description: Two complete page-image PDFs of the 1904 and 1905 EETS parts, digitized from University of Toronto copies and used to inspect title pages, editorial notes, text, and errata.
entities.reproduction_format: Two downloadable page-image PDFs, 282 PDF pages each
notes: These are complete facsimiles of the printed edition, not facsimiles of Add MS 25719.

entities.label: Banks's reduced facsimile of Add MS 25719, f. 131r
entities.description: A single reduced photographic plate printed as the frontispiece to Banks's 1905 Part II. The caption identifies British Museum Additional MS 25,719, leaf 131a, equivalent to modern f. 131r.
entities.reproduction_format: One reduced monochrome photographic plate within the printed edition
notes: This selected image does not satisfy a whole-codex facsimile request.

entities.label: British Library, Add MS 25719
entities.description: The complete surviving witness of the Middle English translation. The work begins on f. 1r, lacks the Latin prologue, and ends on f. 184v with a Latin rhyming scribal colophon; the current catalog separately notes a service-book flyleaf at f. 185.

date_statements.label: Herbert catalog date
date_statements.value: about the middle of the fifteenth century

date_statements.label: current British Library date range
date_statements.notes: The catalog title itself calls the paper manuscript fifteenth-century; its normalized Start/End Date fields are broader.

holding.notes: The translation occupies ff. 1r-184v; f. 185 is a flyleaf from a Latin service book.

physical_description.support: Paper; final flyleaf from a fourteenth-century Latin service book
physical_description.extent: 184 folios for the translation, plus the separately described flyleaf f. 185
notes: Herbert reports 30-41 lines per page.
The current British Library catalog and Herbert agree on paper support; an ARLIMA secondary record's parchment label was not adopted.

entities.label: British Library, Harley MS 268
entities.description: A Latin comparison witness used by Banks for difficult and confused readings. The current catalog places the Alphabetum narrationum at ff. 45r-201r within a larger parchment volume.

date_statements.label: current catalog date
date_statements.value: fourth quarter of the fourteenth century

holding.notes: The Latin Alphabetum occupies ff. 45r-201r; other contents and later additions occupy the remainder of the codex.

notes: The catalog identifies Gothic cursive script and English origin.

entities.label: British Library, Arundel MS 378
entities.description: A Latin comparison witness used by Banks for difficult and confused readings. Herbert describes the complete 117-folio parchment codex as an early-fifteenth-century Alphabetum narrationum formerly belonging to the Carthusians near Mainz.

holding.notes: The Latin Alphabetum occupies ff. 1r-117v and constitutes the codex's described content.

notes: Herbert reports double columns of 40-55 lines and provenance from the Carthusians near Mainz.

entities.label: Arnold of Liege, Alphabetum narrationum
alternate_labels: Alphabetum narrationum of Etienne de Besancon
entities.description: The early-fourteenth-century Latin exemplum compilation translated into Middle English. This conceptual source-work entity does not stand for one identified surviving Latin manuscript or claim that the English translator used Harley MS 268 or Arundel MS 378.
notes: The source edition's Etienne de Besancon attribution is historical and rejected; current specialist sources identify Arnold of Liege.

entities.label: Herbert's 1905 authorship study
entities.description: The article that rejected the historical Etienne de Besancon attribution and assembled the evidence for Arnold of Liege.

bibliographic.citation: J. A. Herbert, 'The Authorship of the Alphabetum Narrationum,' The Library, 2nd ser., 6.21 (1905): 94-101.
bibliographic.title: The Authorship of the Alphabetum Narrationum

entities.label: Miura's study of Latin vocabulary in An Alphabet of Tales
entities.description: A modern linguistic study that compares the Middle English translation with the 2015 critical edition of the Latin source and summarizes the translation's date, unique witness, editorship, and proposed Salley Abbey/Ribble-area provenance.

bibliographic.citation: Ayumi Miura, 'Reception of Latin Vocabulary and Introduction of New Words in An Alphabet of Tales,' Language and Culture Collaborative Research Project 2022 (2023): 20-33.
bibliographic.title: Reception of Latin Vocabulary and Introduction of New Words in An Alphabet of Tales

entities.label: Herbert's Catalogue of Romances, volume 3
entities.description: A scholarly British Museum catalog description of the Latin Alphabetum narrationum and its English translation, including Add MS 25719 on p. 440.

bibliographic.citation: J. A. Herbert, Catalogue of Romances in the Department of Manuscripts in the British Museum, vol. 3 (London: British Museum, 1910).
bibliographic.title: Catalogue of Romances in the Department of Manuscripts in the British Museum

rights.component: The fifteenth-century Middle English translation and embedded medieval Latin matter
rights.basis: The text was created in the fifteenth century and was publicly printed in 1904-1905. Those publication dates are more than the 95-year maximum term described by current U.S. Copyright Office guidance for pre-1978 statutory copyrights. This assessment does not cover modern photographs, catalog text, or electronic markup.

rights.component: Banks's 1904-1905 transcription, notes, editorial interventions, and errata
rights.basis: The two parts were first published in 1904 and 1905, more than the 95-year maximum term described by current U.S. Copyright Office guidance for pre-1978 statutory copyrights; the Internet Archive item metadata also labels both scans NOT_IN_COPYRIGHT.

rights.component: Internet Archive page images and generated derivatives for both scanned volumes
rights.basis: Internet Archive metadata reports no visible copyright notice and possible status NOT_IN_COPYRIGHT for both items, but provides no item-specific open license for the scan derivatives.

rights.component: The reduced manuscript photograph and its printed 1905 plate and caption
rights.basis: The plate was published in Banks's 1905 Part II, more than the 95-year maximum term described by current U.S. Copyright Office guidance for pre-1978 statutory copyrights. This assessment is limited to the historical plate and does not itself license modern scan derivatives.

rights.component: Internet Archive scan derivative of the 1905 plate
rights.basis: The Internet Archive item metadata reports no visible copyright notice and possible status NOT_IN_COPYRIGHT, but provides no item-specific open license for the scan derivatives.

rights.component: U-M electronic transcription and structural markup
rights.jurisdiction: Provider terms and undetermined copyright jurisdiction
rights.basis: The XML permits individual copying for personal use, research, and teaching with the notice retained and permits linking from for-profit works, but expressly forbids redistribution by commercial or not-for-profit content providers. No current item-specific rights label was successfully retrieved during review.
notes: The public-domain status of the medieval text and Banks edition does not by itself remove provider terms or settle rights in the 1997 encoding.

rights.component: The checked-in U-M-derived XML transcription and markup
rights.basis: The repository artifact embeds the same U-M availability and redistribution restriction as the source encoding.

rights.component: Modern photographs, scans, or surrogate images supplied through British Library Imaging Services
rights.basis: The Imaging Services terms make the customer responsible for third-party rights and state that supplied material must not be copied or reproduced without British Library written permission, which may involve additional fees.
notes: Permission to study or privately retain supplied images is not permission to publish them in a Git repository. Obtain item-specific written permission before redistribution.

editorial_practices.source_feature: Obliterated or worn text, inadvertent omissions, manuscript spaces, and abbreviation signs
editorial_practices.representation: Conjectural restorations are bracketed; inadvertent omissions are bracketed and italicized; a manuscript space is distinguished from an omission; final-e signs are represented by small curved strokes, with one diminutive curl after short r omitted.
editorial_practices.description: Banks's Part I Note explicitly defines restoration, supply, bracket, italic, and abbreviation-sign conventions.
Banks states that modern punctuation has been adopted throughout.

editorial_practices.representation: Capitals supplied to personal and place names while some manuscript capitals in common nouns are retained
editorial_practices.description: Banks explicitly distinguishes editorial capitalization of names from selected manuscript capitals retained in common nouns.

editorial_practices.source_feature: Difficult and confused readings in the Middle English manuscript
editorial_practices.representation: Notes quote or compare the Latin copies Harley MS 268 and Arundel MS 378 where they illuminate readings; scribal errors are corrected by footnotes.
editorial_practices.description: Banks's note defines a targeted Latin comparison practice rather than an eclectic replacement text.
editorial_practices.scope: Selected difficult readings throughout Parts I-II

editorial_practices.source_feature: Two separately issued print parts, each with an editorial Note, followed by printed errata
editorial_practices.representation: The two parts are treated as one electronic text; both notes are placed together in front matter, and the errata are encoded but not silently incorporated.
editorial_practices.description: The XML editorial declaration explains the electronic consolidation and treatment of errata.

evidence.citation: General Corpus, CME/source/CME_phase_1-2/AlphTales.xml, header, editorial declaration, full text, explicit, and errata.
evidence.summary: Supplies the local artifact identity and checksum, U-M publication and production statement, complete source citation, historical authorship warning, two-part consolidation, editorial conventions inherited from Banks, provider availability terms, full encoded text, and print-page milestones.

evidence.citation: Reproducible structural inspection of CME/source/CME_phase_1-2/AlphTales.xml.
evidence.locator: DIV1, HEAD, PB, NOTE, and FOREIGN elements
evidence.summary: Counts 806 top-level divisions, 802 numbered tales, 532 page milestones, 1,062 Latin FOREIGN elements, and one French FOREIGN element; confirms opening, closing, explicit, and errata structure.

evidence.citation: Kyvero-Press/CME, AlphTales.xml at commit e1d77c88ca561be924b2fedc54dd474be2e7e49c.
evidence.summary: Provides an exact public raw copy whose SHA-256 matches the checked-out repository artifact.

evidence.citation: University of Michigan Library Digital Collections stable handle for AlphTales.
evidence.summary: The stable handle redirected to U-M's current resolver, but the destination returned an automated Cloudflare challenge during this review, so current interactive text availability and current item-specific rights labeling were not verified.

evidence.citation: Mary Macleod Banks, ed., An Alphabet of Tales, Part I, A-H, EETS Original Series 126 (London: Kegan Paul, Trench, Trubner & Co., 1904), Internet Archive alphabetoftalese01londuoft.
evidence.summary: Establishes Banks's editorship, Add MS 25719 as direct source, Part I extent, and explicit editorial conventions for brackets, italics, supplies, punctuation, capitalization, abbreviation signs, footnotes, and Latin comparison witnesses.

evidence.citation: Mary Macleod Banks, ed., An Alphabet of Tales, Part II, I-Z, EETS Original Series 127 (London: Kegan Paul, Trench, Trubner & Co., 1905), Internet Archive alphabetoftalese02londuoft.
evidence.locator: Frontispiece, title page, Note, pp. 261-532, explicit, and errata
evidence.summary: Establishes Part II extent, its reduced frontispiece facsimile of Add MS 25719, f. 131r, the revised 'once attributed' title, Banks's reliance on Herbert's 1905 authorship article, completion of the text, promised but absent Part III matter, explicit, and errata.

evidence.citation: Mary Macleod Banks, ed., An Alphabet of Tales, Part II, I-Z, EETS Original Series 127 (London, 1905), frontispiece.
evidence.locator: PDF page 2; frontispiece captioned 'Addit. MS. 25,719, Br. Mus., leaf 131a (reduced facsimile)'
evidence.summary: Visual inspection confirms that the plate reproduces one manuscript recto, old-style leaf 131a or modern f. 131r. It is a selected image within the complete printed volume, not a complete facsimile of Add MS 25719.

evidence.citation: Internet Archive items alphabetoftalese01londuoft and alphabetoftalese02londuoft and their metadata APIs.
evidence.summary: Confirms both freely retrievable volume scans, University of Toronto provenance, item identifiers and ARKs, and item metadata stating no visible copyright notice and possible status NOT_IN_COPYRIGHT; neither item supplies an open-license field.

evidence.citation: British Library Archives and Manuscripts Catalogue, Add MS 25719, record 032-002030060.
evidence.locator: Title, scope and content, extent, date, language, digitised content, and access
evidence.summary: Identifies the English translation witness, shelfmark, paper support, missing preface, Latin rhyming colophon, final service-book flyleaf f. 185, and Reader Pass/request route; its Digitised Content field is blank.

evidence.citation: British Library Archives and Manuscripts Catalogue, Harley MS 268, record 040-002046096.
evidence.locator: Contents, date, physical characteristics, and access
evidence.summary: Identifies the Latin Alphabetum at ff. 45r-201r within a 203-folio parchment codex dated to the fourth quarter of the fourteenth century, with English origin and no linked digitised content.

evidence.citation: British Library Archives and Manuscripts Catalogue, Arundel MS 378, record 040-002039661.
evidence.locator: Scope and content, date, extent, and digitised content
evidence.summary: Identifies the 117-folio early-fifteenth-century Alphabetum codex, formerly from the Carthusians near Mainz; the record exposes no digitised-content link.
notes: The current catalog's Language field unexpectedly says English; Herbert and the actual description establish that the Alphabetum text used by Banks is Latin.

evidence.citation: J. A. Herbert, 'The Authorship of the Alphabetum Narrationum,' The Library, 2nd ser., 6.21 (1905): 94-101.
evidence.summary: Rejects Etienne de Besancon, uses the post-1297 and 1308 evidence, the prologue acrostic, Herolt's Arnoldus references, and the Narrator Leodiensis to argue that Arnold of Liege compiled the Latin source.

evidence.citation: J. A. Herbert, Catalogue of Romances in the Department of Manuscripts in the British Museum, vol. 3 (London: British Museum, 1910).
evidence.summary: Describes the Latin collection and witnesses and Add MS 25719 as a mid-fifteenth-century paper manuscript of 184 folios, a Northumbrian translation lacking the prologue, omitting four tales present in Harley 268, and ending with a Latin scribal colophon.

evidence.citation: ARLIMA, 'An alphabet of tales,' record 707.
evidence.summary: Identifies the English prose exemplum translation, Add MS 25719 witness, incipit and explicit, and Banks's 1904-1905 two-volume edition.

evidence.citation: ARLIMA manuscript notice, London, British Library, Add MS 25719, record 9065.
evidence.locator: Digitization status, contents, foliation, and date
evidence.summary: Labels the manuscript undigitized, places the work from f. 1 through its explicit, and records 184 folios and a mid-fifteenth-century date.
notes: Its parchment support statement conflicts with the British Library and Herbert paper descriptions and was not adopted.

evidence.citation: Universidad Complutense de Madrid, MANIPULUS, 'Alphabetum narrationum (1297-1308),' handle 20.500.12238/43.
evidence.summary: Identifies Arnoldus Leodiensis as author, dates the Latin compilation 1297-1308, classifies it as exempla, lists its broad manuscript tradition, and cites the 2015 Brilli edition.

evidence.citation: Ayumi Miura, 'Reception of Latin Vocabulary and Introduction of New Words in An Alphabet of Tales,' Language and Culture Collaborative Research Project 2022 (2023): 20-33.
evidence.summary: Describes An Alphabet of Tales as an anonymous mid-fifteenth-century Northern English translation surviving uniquely in Add MS 25719, reports the Salley Abbey/Ribble-area provenance proposal from Johnson, identifies Banks as the only complete edition, and notes that the planned Part III was never published.

evidence.citation: British Library, Digitisation services.
evidence.locator: Online ordering, required information, formats, delivery, colour, and permissions
evidence.summary: Provides the public order form and rate card, requires shelfmark and recto/verso folio details, distinguishes reference images from reproduction photography, states that original manuscripts are not supplied as ordinary scans, advises explicitly requesting colour, and directs reuse enquiries to licensing staff.

evidence.citation: British Library, Imaging Services terms and conditions, updated January 2026.
evidence.locator: Preservation, turnaround, pricing, and copyright and reproductions
evidence.summary: States that difficult or large orders can fall outside standard prices and times, places third-party rights clearance on the customer, and requires written British Library permission before copying or reproducing supplied material.

evidence.citation: British Library, Digitisation and reproduction services price list, 2025/26 rates.
evidence.summary: Lists standard first and subsequent image prices for reference scans and reproduction photography, microfilm charges, and advanced imaging; the document warns that alternatives and large projects can incur different costs.

evidence.citation: U.S. Copyright Office, Circular 15A, Duration of Copyright, revised April 2026.
evidence.locator: p. 2, works already under statutory protection before 1978
evidence.summary: Describes a 95-year maximum total term for pre-1978 statutory copyrights and states that U.S.-published works before 1 January 1931 are public domain, supporting the jurisdiction-specific duration analysis for Banks's 1904-1905 edition.

evidence.citation: Date-bounded public facsimile search for British Library Add MS 25719.
evidence.summary: On 2026-07-11, exact-shelfmark searches across the British Library catalog and digitised-manuscript guidance, general web indexes, ARLIMA, and likely IIIF routes found no retrievable complete Add MS 25719 facsimile. Banks's print edition provides one reduced image of f. 131r, while catalog descriptions and mediated ordering routes cover the complete codex.
notes: This records search scope and date, not proof that no internal, private, or unindexed surrogate exists.

open_questions.question: Does the British Library hold an existing complete digital or microfilm surrogate of Add MS 25719, and can a complete physical-codex copy be supplied with reusable item-specific terms?
next_steps: Ask Imaging Services whether a complete surrogate already exists and request the entire codex rather than only ff. 1r-184v.
Specify Add MS 25719, every recto and verso through f. 185, covers and unfoliated leaves where reproducible, colour, file format, resolution, and preservation of the original sequence.
Ask permissions@bl.uk in writing whether the supplied images may be redistributed in the repository and under what credit, fee, and license conditions.
If supplied, cache one complete deliverable or one auditable full-image bundle and map An Alphabet of Tales to ff. 1r-184v with start and end image links.

open_questions.question: Which Latin manuscript or textual branch supplied the anonymous Middle English translation?
next_steps: Consult the 2015 Brilli edition and later transmission studies for variant evidence linking the English version to the Latin tradition.
Do not convert Banks's editorial comparison of Harley 268 and Arundel 378 into a medieval exemplar claim.

open_questions.question: How secure are the translation's conventional c. 1440-c. 1450 date and the Salley Abbey/Ribble-area provenance proposal reported by Miura from Johnson?
next_steps: Obtain lawful access to Johnson's full dissertation and evaluate its linguistic data against current localization resources.
Inspect the complete manuscript or facsimile for codicological evidence that separates translation date, copy date, and manuscript origin.
notes: Miura directly reports Johnson's proposal, but Johnson's restricted dissertation file was not accessed during this review.

open_questions.question: What current item-specific access and rights statement does U-M apply to AlphTales, and does it alter or clarify the redistribution restriction embedded in the 1997 XML?
next_steps: Retry the U-M item in an ordinary interactive browser or ask U-M Digital Collections for the current rights record.
Preserve the embedded restriction until the provider supplies a clear item-specific interpretation; do not infer redistribution permission from the public-domain print source.
